perturbation.original_text: Borrower and Lending Agent hereby hereby irrevocably waive any and all rights to trial by jury in any legal proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Borrower and Lending Agent may consider waiving any and all rights to trial by jury in any legal proceeding arising out of or relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the jury waiver from a mandatory to a permissive clause reduces its enforceability and contradicts the intent of a clear waiver.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 7th Amendment to the United States Constitution
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification undermines the clarity of the jury waiver. The 7th Amendment guarantees the right to a jury trial in civil cases. While this right can be waived, the waiver must be knowing and voluntary. Changing 'hereby irrevocably waive' to 'may consider waiving' introduces ambiguity, potentially invalidating the waiver. Some jurisdictions may interpret this as insufficient to constitute a valid waiver, thereby contradicting the intent of ensuring a clear and enforceable waiver of the constitutional right to a jury trial.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Seventh Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Seventh Amendment The Seventh Amendment continues a practice from English common law of distinguishing civil claims which must be tried before a jury (absent waiver by the parties) from claims and issues that may be heard by a judge alone. It only governs federal civil courts and has no application to civil courts set up by the states when those courts are hearing only disputes of state law . Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment VII In suits at common law, where the value in controversy shall exceed twenty dollars, the right of trial by jury shall be preserved, and no fact tried by a jury, shall be otherwise reexamined in any court of the United States, than according to the rules of the common law. Wex Resources Criminal Law Criminal Procedure Jury Other Resources US Code: Title 18 Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure ‹ Sixth Amendment up Eighth Amendment › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by weakening a mandatory jury waiver, potentially rendering it unenforceable. The original text states "hereby irrevocably waive," which is a clear and decisive waiver. The changed text says "may consider waiving," introducing ambiguity. The Seventh Amendment, as described in Scraped Law Snippet 2, preserves the right to trial by jury, 'absent waiver by the parties'. By making the waiver discretionary, the modified contract jeopardizes the validity of the waiver, conflicting with the intent to ensure a clear relinquishment of the constitutional right to a jury trial.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of New York, without regard to its conflict of law principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the principles of fairness, without regard to any specific laws or conflict of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the defined jurisdiction with vague 'principles of fairness' makes the contract unenforceable due to lack of legal clarity.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change introduces a lack of clarity by replacing the specification of New York law with vague 'principles of fairness'. New York General Obligations Law § 5-1401 allows parties to a contract involving at least $250,000 to specify New York law as governing. By removing the reference to New York law, the modified text contradicts the clear and predictable choice of law provisions allowed under New York law, potentially leading to uncertainty and disputes over which jurisdiction's law applies.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-1401 Choice of law General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 14 § 5-1401. Choice of law. 1. The parties to any contract, agreement or undertaking, contingent or otherwise, in consideration of, or relating to any obligation arising out of a transaction covering in the aggregate not less than two hundred fifty thousand dollars, including a transaction otherwise covered by subsection (a) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code, may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties in whole or in part, whether or not such contract, agreement or undertaking bears a reasonable relation to this state. This section shall not apply to any contract, agreement or undertaking (a) for labor or personal services, (b) relating to any transaction for personal, family or household services, or (c) to the extent provided to the contrary in subsection (c) of section 1--301 of the uniform commercial code. 2. Nothing contained in this section shall be construed to limit or deny the enforcement of any provision respecting choice of law in any other contract, agreement or undertaking.
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the specified jurisdiction of 'the laws of the State of New York' with 'the principles of fairness'. This directly contradicts the ability for parties to specify New York law as governing their agreement, as outlined in NY General Obligations Law § 5-1401. The scraped law snippet from NYSenate.gov explicitly states in SECTION 5-1401 that 'The parties to any contract...may agree that the law of this state shall govern their rights and duties'. By removing the reference to New York law and instead referencing general principles, the modified text undermines this legal provision, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Borrower shall maintain the Information in confidence and shall not disclose the information to any third party without the consent of Lending Agent; provided, however, that Borrower may disclose the Information to the extent required by Sample 2 subpoena, court order, request from a regulatory body with jurisdiction over Borrower, or as otherwise required by law or regulation.
perturbation.changed_text: Borrower shall have the option to maintain the Information in confidence and may disclose the information to any third party as Borrower deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the confidentiality requirement and exceptions undermines the core purpose of the non-disclosure agreement, rendering it useless.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the confidentiality clause and allowing disclosure at Borrower's discretion, the modified text effectively nullifies the purpose of the NDA. New York law, protects trade secrets and confidential information. This change allows for unrestricted disclosure, directly conflicting with legal protections for confidential business information. In addition to protection of trade secrets, protection of confidential information is ensured by CPLR § 4548, which allows self-authentication of copies of records for use as evidence so that one can prove breach of confidentially.

Privileged communications; electronic communication thereof. No communication privileged under this article shall lose its privileged character for the sole reason that it is communicated by electronic means or because persons necessary for the delivery or facilitation of such electronic communication may have access to the content of the communication.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates confidentiality of information with specific exceptions, while the changed text provides the borrower the option to maintain confidentiality and allows disclosure at their discretion. This directly contradicts the purpose of an NDA, which aims to protect confidential information. The law explanation mentions that New York law protects trade secrets and confidential information, and this change allows for unrestricted disclosure, conflicting with legal protections for confidential business information. Although the scraped snippets do not directly address trade secrets laws, the removal of confidentiality obligations undermines the protections typically afforded to confidential information under common law and potentially statutory provisions regarding trade secrets, thus creating a legal risk for the lending agent.

perturbation.original_text: If Borrower (or its employees or agents) breaches or threatens to breach this Agreement, Lending Agent may, in addition to any other remedies available, seek any equitable remedy it deems appropriate to enjoin an actual or threatened violation by Borrower.
perturbation.changed_text: If Borrower (or its employees or agents) breaches or threatens to breach this Agreement, Lending Agent may, at its sole discretion, send a strongly worded letter to Borrower.
perturbation.explanation: Restricting the remedy for breach to a 'strongly worded letter' significantly weakens the enforcement power of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Civil Practice Law and Rules (CPLR) Article 63
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification significantly weakens the remedies available to the Lending Agent in case of a breach. New York CPLR Article 63 allows for injunctive relief in cases where a party is threatened with irreparable harm. By limiting the remedy to only a 'strongly worded letter', the clause contradicts the availability of standard legal remedies, such as injunctions, that would otherwise be available under New York law to prevent or stop a breach of contract. This contradicts CPLR § 6301, which allows for preliminary injunctions and temporary restraining orders.

Grounds for preliminary injunction and temporary restraining order. A preliminary injunction may be granted in any action where it appears that the defendant threatens or is about to do, or is doing or procuring or suffering to be done, an act in violation of the plaintiff's rights respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual, or in any action where the plaintiff has demanded and would be entitled to a judgment restraining the defendant from the commission or continuance of an act, which, if committed or continued during the pendency of the action, would produce injury to the plaintiff. A temporary restraining order may be granted pending a hearing for a preliminary injunction where it appears that immediate and irreparable injury, loss or damage will result unless the defendant is restrained before the hearing can be had.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows the Lending Agent to seek equitable remedies for a breach, while the changed text limits the remedy to only sending a 'strongly worded letter'. This directly contradicts the availability of standard legal remedies, such as injunctions, as supported by NY CPLR § 6301, which states that a preliminary injunction may be granted to restrain a defendant from violating the plaintiff's rights. The change weakens the enforcement power, making it a structural flaw that creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement and understanding between the parties and supersedes any and all prior agreements and understandings, oral or written, relating to th e subject matter hereof. This Agreement may not be amended except by a writing signed by both parties hereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes a suggestion of an agreement and understanding between the parties. This Agreement may be amended orally and without any written documentation.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the 'entire agreement' clause to a 'suggestion' and allowing oral amendments destroys the integrity of the written contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification significantly weakens the enforceability of the agreement. New York General Obligations Law § 15-301 addresses the effectiveness of no-oral-modification clauses in written agreements. By stating that the agreement may be amended orally, the clause contradicts the protection offered by NY GOL § 15-301, which makes no-oral-modification clauses enforceable. The change also negates the intent of an integration clause, making the agreement subject to potentially unenforceable oral agreements.

General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 15, TITLE 3 § 15-301. When written agreement or other instrument cannot be changed by oral executory agreement, or discharged or terminated by oral executory agreement or oral consent or by oral notice. 1. A written agreement or other written instrument which contains a provision to the effect that it cannot be changed orally, cannot be changed by an executory agreement unless such executory agreement is in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement of the change is sought or by his agent. 2. A written agreement or other written instrument which contains a provision to the effect that it cannot be terminated orally, cannot be discharged by an executory agreement unless such executory agreement is in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement of the discharge is sought, or by his agent, and cannot be terminated by mutual consent unless such termination is effected by an executed accord and satisfaction other than the substitution of one executory contract for another, or is evidenced by a writing signed by the party against whom it is sought to enforce the termination, or by his agent. 3. a. A discharge or partial discharge of obligations under a written agreement or other written instrument is a change of the agreement or instrument for the purpose of subdivision one of this section and is not a discharge or termination for the purpose of subdivision two, unless all executory obligations under the agreement or instrument are discharged or terminated. b. A discharge or termination of all executory obligations under a written agreement or other written instrument is a discharge or termination for the purpose of subdivision two even though accrued obligations remaining unperformed at the date of the discharge or termination are not affected by it. c. If a
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts New York General Obligations Law § 15-301, which enforces no-oral-modification clauses in written agreements. The original text contains an 'entire agreement' clause and prohibits oral amendments. The changed text states the agreement is merely a 'suggestion' and allows oral amendments, thus undermining the enforceability and integrity of the written contract, which is in direct conflict with NY GOL § 15-301's intent to prevent oral modifications of written agreements. The snippet from NYSenate.gov explicitly states, 'A written agreement or other written instrument which contains a provision to the effect that it cannot be changed orally, cannot be changed by an executory agreement unless such executory agreement is in writing and signed by the party against whom enforcement of the change is sought or by his agent,' which is exactly what the changed text violates.
